question.text: An investment grew from Rs. 1,00,000 to Rs. 1,21,000 over 2 years. The simple annualized return is 10.5% while the CAGR is 10%. Which measure is more appropriate for comparing investment performance?

answer.text: Simple annualized return because it's higher
CAGR because it accounts for the compounding effect
Both are equally valid for comparison

explanation.text: **The Logic:** CAGR is the accepted standard measure for comparing investment returns because it accounts for compounding. Simple return overstates performance by ignoring the compounding effect. CAGR gives the underlying rate at which the investment grew. **The Trap:** Higher simple return doesn't mean better — CAGR provides the true compound growth rate. **Cross-Exam Context:** CAGR is the standard return measure in mutual fund fact sheets (V-A, V-B) and performance evaluation (X-A, XXI-B). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** SEBI mandates CAGR for periods over 1 year in mutual fund scheme advertisements — always use CAGR for apples-to-apples comparison.
